data.title: 2 Investment-Grade Baby Bonds For You And Me
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

In our recent articles written for our followers and readers on Seeking Alpha, we have been discussing the immunity that fixed-income securities offer with respect to everyday market sentiment and the news cycle. While they are less correlated with common equity and offer lower volatility, these assets are not entirely shielded from all economic parameters. Their prices tend to be influenced by prevailing interest rates (and sentiment around rates), the creditworthiness of the issuer, duration to maturity, and the coupon.

  • Price sensitivity: Fixed-income tends to be very sensitive to interest rate changes. Long-duration bonds, in particular, are more sensitive to interest rate movements. If rates fall after you buy, the price of the bond will rise significantly, giving you capital gains on top of your income. Conversely, if rates rise after you buy, it can create unrealized losses despite producing the income you paid to secure.

  • Credit quality: Investment-grade bonds are rated this way because they have lower default risk. This means that, as an investor, you're mainly exposed to interest rate and duration risk, not credit risk.

  • Duration risk: Duration measures a bond’s sensitivity to interest rate changes – the longer the duration, the more its price will fluctuate when rates move. This means long-duration bonds are more exposed to interest rate risk, potentially leading to larger price swings.

  • Coupon at par: Fixed-income securities issued when interest rates are high typically carry higher coupons than those issued during low-rate environments. Over time, as interest rates move through cycles, prices adjust to prevailing market conditions. Higher-coupon securities are more likely to trade at a premium (above par) when rates fall, while lower-coupon securities may experience steeper price declines relative to higher-coupon issues during periods of rising rates.

Normally, long-duration bonds are not the most favorable investments, but we are amidst rare circumstances where elevated interest rates have pushed some high-quality long-duration bonds to deeply discounted territory. Moreover, their low coupon at par makes them less likely to be called early by the issuer, allowing investors to lock in +6.5% yields for the foreseeable future.

“Opportunities come infrequently. When it rains gold, put out the bucket, not the thimble.” – Warren Buffett

Let’s dive into our top investment-grade bonds for steady income through the market noise.

Pick #1: AFG Baby Bonds: Up To 7.1% Yields

American Financial Group (AFG) is a diversified financial services company formed in 1959, but whose insurance roots trace back to the 1872-founded Great American Insurance Company. AFG offers specialized property and casualty (P&C) insurance products to businesses across various industries. The Specialty P&C insurance operations generated a combined ratio of 89.0 for FY 2024. Net written premiums during FY 2024 grew by 7% YoY. AFG maintains an excellent balance sheet, rated A+/A1 by leading credit agencies, and the company has maintained this rating for 115+ years in a row!

AFG has grown its common stock dividends for 19 years in a row, and has notably paid two special dividends in 2023 and two in 2024. In addition, AFG has also pursued massive share buyback programs, demonstrating its commitment to deliver value for shareholders. The company has consistently outperformed the S&P 500 over the past 3 decades.

AFG generated $887 million in net earnings in FY 2024, while spending $76 million on interest expenses. The company ended FY 2024 with $1.4 billion in cash and cash equivalents. The company has four public baby bonds, carrying BBB+ credit ratings from AM Best. All AFG baby bonds currently offer comparable yields of ~7%. Yet, the lower coupon issue AFGE offers a 6.9% yield and ~52% upside to par. All baby bonds will become callable from September 2025, but only mature in 2059/2060, making them long-duration and more sensitive to interest rate changes.

  • 5.875% Subordinated Debentures due March 30, 2059 (AFGB) – Yield 7.1%
  • 5.125% Subordinated Debentures due December 15, 2059 (AFGC) –Yield 6.9%
  • 5.625% Subordinated Debentures due June 1, 2060 (AFGD) – Yield 7.1%
  • 4.50% Subordinated Debentures due September 15, 2060 (AFGE) –Yield 6.9%

Its lowest issuance coupon AFGE is less likely to be redeemed after the call date, while AFGB is most likely to be redeemed due to its highest coupon in the group.

Remember that AFG bonds only mature in 2059/2060, so they can be very volatile from the effects of changing interest rate cycles, but attractive for long-term income investors.

Pick #2: KKR Baby Bond: Yield 6.6%

KKR Inc. (KKR) is a leading asset manager specializing in alternative assets, including private equity, real estate, infrastructure, credit, and hedge funds. The company ended 2024 with ~$660 billion in assets under management.

  • 4.625% Subordinated Notes due 4/1/2061 (KKRS) – Yield 6.6%

KKR has an A-rated balance sheet, with $4.2 billion in cash and short-term investments. The company has one baby bond – KKRS, which currently offers a 6.6% yield and ~42% upside to par. The baby bond carries a BBB+ credit rating. However, with a 4.625% coupon and a 2059 maturity date, investors must be prepared for price volatility across interest-rate cycles.

KKR ended the fiscal year with ~$4.9 billion in total income, calculated after spending $271.1 million on interest payments, indicating solid coverage. Needless to say, the company’s baby bond presents a healthy means to secure a high-quality yield from a massive industry leader.

Conclusion

At the beginning of this article, we discussed a few parameters that influence the trading price of fixed-income securities. Long-term income investors can play these parameters to their advantage and lock lucrative yields for years, if not decades. At High Dividend Opportunities, we hold over 45 individual preferred stocks and baby bonds, to collect income from an attractive mix of credit quality, duration, coupon, and issuer industry.

When interest rates are higher, long-duration and low-coupon preferreds and bonds will experience selling pressure, resulting in discounted pricing and higher current yields. Of course, interest rates are not going to change course immediately just because you bought into the investment, but your income begins to flow right away.

You can collect steady income regardless of the market price action, and can sleep well at night doing so – given the excellent credit quality of the issuers and their fundamental strength in the industry. Let others time the market. You’ll be too busy collecting checks – and sleeping soundly while doing it.

This is the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Sibanye Stillwater's Turnaround Ignites, The Upside Is Still There
data.description:
Intro & Thesis

I initiated my investment coverage of Sibanye Stillwater Limited (NYSE:SBSW) in late May 2024 with a "Buy" rating, arguing that the cyclicality was about to turn. Then I updated mt rating in mid-September last year after SBSW corrected sharply; at the time, I thought that SBSW had likely found the bottom of its correction and that it was destined to rise up with even slight positives coming from its underlying commodities markets. I also argued that SBSW's cost-cutting measures should help it boost its bottom line meaningfully as the PGM metal pricing stabilizes.

As time has shown, my bullish thesis played out well eventually as SBSW's nominal price is up 45% since my thesis update, beating the broader market with a hefty margin:

From what I see right now on SBSW's main markets, I think that the stock's rally is very likely to keep going further. The revisions recently made to Sibanye's EPS led to a massive drop in its forward price-to-earnings multiples and valuation in general, supporting continuing strength in SBSW's stock price action, so I keep my "Buy" rating unchanged for now.

Why Do I Think So?

Obviously, the FY2024 results for Sibanye haven't been great as their revenue went down by over 4% YoY and gross profit continued to slide lower, losing over 51% on a YoY basis (Seeking Alpha data, in US dollar terms). Selling price was the key driver of that deterioration: The basket price at the South African PGM operations fell by 16% YoY, at the US PGM operations by 21% YoY, and at the US PGM recycling operations by ~46% YoY. But what helped Sibanye limit the negative impact below gross profit was their continuing cost reduction measures, so the firm closed FY2024 with a great improvement on the EBIT level (the FY2023 loss was ~4x higher), and despite higher net interest expenses for the period, the resulting bottom line showed a great reversal (although it stayed in the negative territory, obviously).

The 2H FY2024 EBITDA of ZAR 6.4 billion "marked the third consecutive six-month period of stable EBITDA", as the outgoing CEO Neal Froneman noted on the earnings call, and looking at the past 2 semiannual periods, we see SBSW's gradual normalization in EBITDA generation through better diversification.

Delving into the particular segments, I see that Sibanye's SA gold operations showed a spectacular +216% YoY increase in 2H FY2024, contributing ZAR 3.5 billion in H2 alone and just under ZAR 6 billion for the full year. At the current PGM prices, the SA gold operations should generate more than half of the group's EBITDA in 2025, and the incoming CEO, Richard Stewart, said that despite being mature, this segment's assets are well-capitalized and do not require massive CAPEX injections, which is good news.

SA PGM operations showed a 16% YoY decline in the 4E PGM basket prices, as I noted above, so this segment's adjusted EBITDA dropped by about 60% to ZAR 7.5 billion for FY2024. But some of that decline was also exacerbated by a seemingly one-off event: No revenue and earnings were recognized from Kroondal for the last 4 months of FY2024 "due to pipeline build-up", so FY2025 SA PGM results should be much better from this fact alone, in my understanding.

Regarding US PGM operations, the 21% YoY decline in the 2E PGM basket price was a heavy hit, and in addition to that, this segment booked ~$500 million impairment "due to lower long-term palladium price forecasts". The new management wants to keep pushing the all-in sustaining costs (AISC) lower (they plan to reach $1,000/oz in 3 years; now they're at $1,367/2Eoz), so if they do achieve this target, the absence of impairment in future period should theoretically boost the consolidated bottom line.

The newly acquired Reldan, a part of Sibanye's recycling US operations, contributed ~$32 million to the consolidated FY2024 EBITDA, and I think it's just the beginning because the catalytic converter recycling market size is expected to reach ~$16.02 billion by 2032, growing at a CAGR of 6.8%, according to a third-party source.

Overall, I think Sibanye's cost management was well-executed and helped the firm weather the weakness of the ASPs; now that platinum and palladium are projected to be in deficit this year (UBS data proprietary source, May 2025), the PGM prices are likely to normalize further, reigniting SBSW's top line recovery.

Sibanye's reported net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio was 1.79x as of 2H 2024, with the pro-forma net debt to adjusted EBITDA - "after accounting for the $500 million precious metals stream agreement" (primarily on SA PGM gold byproducts) - dropped to <1.1x. At the same time, Sibanye's liquidity looks strong after it raised ZAR 35.8 billion over 12 months. Also, the Section 45X tax credits under the Inflation Reduction Act, "for 10% of operating costs for critical minerals production and recycling," should give Sibanye massive direct cash payments, effectively lowering their AISC of the US operations.

The more favorable outlook for ASPs and AISC likely led to the massive revisions regarding SBSW's EPS for FY2026; since late January 2025, the consensus FY2026 EPS went up from $0.04 to $0.64. The FY2027 consensus doubled from late February 2025.

Currently, SBSW is trading at a 9.38x FY2025 P/E ratio and less than 5x its FY2026 P/E. At the same time, all Seeking Alpha Quant ratings have reversed from "F" and "D" to "B" and even "A". The cycle might have finally turned around.

While I admit that cyclical names usually trade at a steep discount to what is considered normal multiples, SBSW's FY2026 P/E of under 5x is simply too low to be true. There should be a repricing process, in my view, if the recent revisions are there to stay. I think it's fair to assume that SBSW might keep its 7-9x earnings multiple by the end of FY2026. Then, at the current EPS consensus (no premium or discount added), the stock price target amounts to $9.84 based on my calculations.

My calculated price target coincides well with the nearest resistance level, if the stock price manages to hold above the $5-6/share level.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

As I noted in my previous article, the key risks to my bullish thesis are tied to the trend in the auto market, the ICE-to-EV transition, which is indicating decreased demand for palladium and platinum in the very long term. I know PGMs are used outside of the auto industry as well, but in 2020, ~60% of global PGMs were utilized for autocatalysts, according to Visual Capitalist. So it's the key market, and as EVs replace ICEs, the demand should stay under pressure.

Another risk is cyclicality. Current trends suggest that SBSW is undergoing a change in its industry's cycle, which is essentially the core of my thesis update today. But if the tide isn't turning and we are merely seeing short-term relief, SBSW might continue to reach new lows. My technical analysis should also be considered with a skeptical eye.

Final Thoughts

Despite the above risks, I think Sibanye Stillwater is growing for a reason, and its upside potential is much higher than what it has managed to achieve in recent weeks. My valuation and price target calculations for 2026 suggest SBSW's price at ~$9.84, giving potential buyers an upside of >64%. My technical analysis agrees - SBSW should find stable ground above $5-6/share to try to conquer the next resistance level.

Given all that, I reiterate my "Buy" rating on SBSW stock.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: Pan American Silver Or Pan American Gold?
data.description:
Additional Thoughts On Silver

As discussed in a previous article about the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) and the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV), I am highly optimistic about silver prices over the long term. In a rapidly electrifying world, structural trends support demand for silver. In contrast, according to the 2025 World Silver Survey, mine production has declined by about 10% since 2016. So with these two intersecting trends, why is silver still trading below $35.00 an ounce?

More and more people are starting to understand the fiscal death spiral in which the United States and many other Western countries find themselves. If the perspective around government bonds is shifting, where is the safety? What if the answer resides in hard assets, including precious metals like gold and silver? Silver: A Strategic Industrial And Monetary Metal In Structural Deficit

Before the 2008 Global Financial Crisis, the U.S. federal debt was still below $10 trillion. After a historic surge following the COVID-19 pandemic, the $37 trillion threshold should be crossed in the coming weeks. After an almost 4x increase in the national debt, why are silver prices still so far below their highs of ~$50 reached in April 2011?

2025 will mark the 5th year in a row of silver seeing a hefty deficit which, for 2021-25, totals almost 800Moz. This is clearly unsustainable and so we set out below what may lie in store. We cannot expect much of a solution to this deficit position from mine production as this is expected to peak in 2026 and then to fall as several mines start to reach their end-of-life [...] Recycling will also be constrained by photographic scrap’s structural decline and by depleted silverware stocks in the West [...] At the same time, we remain optimistic for industrial demand. 2025 World Silver Survey

What if we return to the spring of 1980, when the Hunt brothers attempted to corner the silver market? In this era, the U.S. federal debt was still below $1 trillion, and in January 1980, silver prices reached their intraday COMEX all-time high of $50.35 per ounce. Since then, the U.S. federal debt has increased by almost 40x, but silver prices are still 35% below their highs. Considering the monetary debasement of the last decades, silver prices in real terms are even more depressing to look at. To make matters even worse, the gold-to-silver ratio has hovered around 100:1 for a few weeks now, a level that has only been visited a few times throughout history.

So Bujon, why are you so bullish on silver? The answer is simple: it is precisely because of the abovementioned reasons. For decades, silver has probably been one of the most depressing assets to own, and I am perfectly fine with that. My investment horizon is measured in decades, and I am perfectly positioned to use market weaknesses to boost my purchasing power.

When I look at the geopolitical landscape, I see serious vulnerabilities surrounding the U.S. Treasury market. How many millions of retirees in the Western world are invested in 60/40 portfolios? As a young man, I ask myself: Why would I save Dollars when they can print an unlimited amount of them at will and at no cost? When the money printing machine is used, the closer you are to the printing press, the more you benefit. Considering my lifestyle in the woods, I am obviously very far away from the printing press, hence the need for hard assets and currencies.

In my 20s, I was excited to play the Dollar game, and living in Toronto provided me with a fantastic life experience. However, I will turn 30 next month, and after living for three years in the woods, I do not want to run on the Dollar treadmill anymore. Some will say that the solution is Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies in general, but considering their total dependence upon the electric grid, I would rather own a metal like silver - a hybrid between a pure industrial metal like copper and a pure precious metal like gold - the best of both worlds.

The question then becomes, what is the best way to gain exposure to silver prices? In my last article, I looked into First Majestic Silver (AG). What I saw was a company printing a tremendous amount of paper while deploying the freshly raised capital into questionable acquisitions like Jerritt Canyon. This is where Pan American Silver (NYSE:PAAS) enters the story.

To put things in perspective, Jerritt Canyon represented approximately 21% of AG's revenues in 2022. We are now more than two years into the 'temporary suspension', and there is still no clear plan to restart production. In 2024, First Majestic completed limited work on the property, while an 18,000-meter drilling program is planned for 2025. What was bought as a cash-generating asset is now a cash-consuming asset. The price tag? 26.7 million shares. First Majestic Silver: Printing Paper Even Faster Than The Federal Reserve

Pan American Silver Or Pan American Gold?

Throughout 2024, Pan American Silver produced 21.1 million ounces of silver, making it the fifth-largest silver producer in the world. As illustrated in the table below, Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF) and KGHM Polska (OTCPK:KGHPF) remain the industry giants, with a combined production of almost 100 million ounces per year.

According to the 2025 World Silver Survey, primary mine production amounted to ~835 million ounces in 2024, meaning that Fresnillo and KGHM Polska have a combined market share of around 12%. In this sense, I would say that silver production is highly fragmented and largely inelastic. In the event of a large increase in spot prices, no silver producer can take the lead and boost production, and even if they could, silver is primarily mined as a by-product. If we think about a large copper mine producing silver as a by-product, even a massive surge in silver prices would be unlikely to change materially the long-term economics of the mine. Even if it could, I doubt it would be prudent to deploy additional capital and ramp up production based solely on the price of a by-product.

If we go back to Pan American Silver, we can see its large silver production on an absolute basis, but the company's portfolio also includes meaningful gold production. Based on the most recent guidance, the gold segment is expected to produce between 735,000 and 800,000 ounces in 2025. Using estimates from RBC Capital Markets, Pan American Silver is expected to generate approximately 27% of its 2025 revenues from silver, and that includes the MAG Silver (MAG) acquisition announced earlier this month. Without MAG's 44% interest in the world-class Juanicipio Mine, silver would have accounted for ~20% of Pan American's total revenues.

In this context, the name 'Pan American Silver' is somewhat misleading, as we are dealing with a relatively large gold producer instead. In my view, a name change should have been announced soon after the Yamana's transaction, which drastically altered Pan American's portfolio weightings.

To be clear, I do not view Pan American's move towards gold as good or bad, but with less than 30% of its revenues expected to come from silver in 2025, it is simply not the ideal investment vehicle to expose oneself to spot silver prices. I am also highly optimistic about gold prices, it is simply that I am currently shopping for silver exposure.

The Battle Between Share Issuances & Production Volumes

If we go back a decade, Pan American Silver ended 2015 with a production of 26.1 million ounces of silver and 183,700 ounces of gold. In contrast, PAAS is expected to produce 20.5 million ounces of silver and 767,500 ounces of gold at the midpoint of the 2025 guidance range. In other words, Pan American's silver production has declined by ~20%, and gold production has increased by ~320% over the last decade.

Production volumes do not mean very much in themselves, as any management team could easily increase production by selling shares to pursue acquisitions. However, when the number of shares outstanding is juxtaposed to production volumes, I think it reveals a great deal about long-term capital allocation. Any mining company must deal with depletion and production decline rates; this is a fact of nature. The real question is about how efficiently those ounces are replaced, and this is where skills enter the picture.

A great promoter would have no trouble increasing production, but the truth will inevitably be revealed in the number of shares outstanding. On the other hand, a judicious capital allocator may increase production while keeping the number of shares outstanding flat or even in decline if buybacks are pursued.

Pan American Silver ended 2015 with 151.6 million fully diluted shares outstanding, compared to 363.5 million as of the first quarter of 2025. To recapitulate the last few paragraphs, we get a ~20% decrease in silver production and a ~320% increase in gold production juxtaposed to a ~140% increase in fully diluted shares outstanding. I do not have enough knowledge to comment on how this performance ranks among the numerous other precious metals producers, but I know for a fact that it is much better than what First Majestic Silver delivered over the same period.

First Majestic ended 2016 with a production volume of 18.7 million silver-equivalent ounces compared to a 2025 production guidance of 29.5 million silver-equivalent ounces at the midpoint. While the number of shares outstanding increased by almost 300%, production volumes increased by only ~60%. That's my core issue with investing my hard-earned capital in First Majestic's shares. First Majestic Silver: Printing Paper Even Faster Than The Federal Reserve

These are solid results, especially considering that ~56 million shares were issued during the Tahoe acquisition back in February 2019. The Escobal Mine in Guatemala was one of Tahoe's key assets, and despite hosting 265 million ounces of proven and probable silver, the mine remains halted following a prolonged conflict with local communities. Over the long run, I see it as unlikely that such a great mineral endowment, with all the infrastructure already in place, remains untapped.

As illustrated above, Escobal produced 21 million ounces of silver in 2016, which is exactly what Pan American's overall portfolio produced in 2025. When it comes to timing, Escobal's contingent value right (OTCPK:PAASF) expires in 2029, and nobody in 2017 could have imagined that Escobal would still be halted almost ten years later, in 2025. If I have learned a hard lesson over the last few years, it is precisely that events can take much longer to materialize than anything I could have imagined initially. Time decay is my main issue with holding out-of-the-money financial instruments with a predetermined expiration date. In this sense, I would much rather own Pan American's common shares with their quasi-perpetual option on Escobal's restart embedded in them.

Conclusion

The last element I would like to mention surrounding Pan American is the short economic lives of some assets. For example, the Dolores Mine in Mexico ceased mining activities in July 2024 and completed stacking in January 2025. Another case would be the Minera Florida Mine in Chile, which has only ~315,000 ounces of proven and probable gold left. The Cerro Morro Mine in Argentina is not far behind, with 250,000 ounces of proven and probable gold remaining. Pan American may find some ways to extend their economic lives by a few more years, but the depletion treadmill never stops.

From a capital allocation perspective, Pan American did a much better job than First Majestic over the last decade or so. However, over the last few years, both companies have significantly underperformed gold and silver prices. Anyone exposed to precious metals via miners must assume the basis risk between the two asset classes. Although Escobal's restart could act as an important catalyst, Pan American must also deal with a pretty steep production decline in the coming years.

My 'Hold' rating reflects my lack of excitement about Pan American. Based on my knowledge about the company today, I would prefer to own the metals directly and wait for a truly great opportunity to arise. There are so many risks with miners; hence the importance of making sure I am properly compensated to warehouse them on my personal balance sheet.

data.title: Avoid These Dividend Disasters Before It's Too Late
data.description:
Written by Jussi Askola for High Yield Investor.

We live in a very challenging environment, and I fear that many dividend investors are ill-prepared for what we are facing.

Interest rates are still high. The trade war is raging. And before you know, the AI revolution will start to ravage big parts of the economy.

If you are ignoring these important themes, you are setting yourself up for disaster.

A lot of dividend stocks may seem compelling on the surface, but most of them are at greater risk than the market today realizes.

In what follows, I will discuss each of these themes one by one and highlight some popular dividend stocks that could be at risk of a cut.

High Interest Rates

In late 2023, the Fed began to cut interest rates, and this led to renewed confidence in many high-yielding sectors. REITs (VNQ), as an example, have surged by 35% since then:

And that's just the average of the sector. There are many individual REITs that are up much more than that. For instance, the highly leveraged mall REIT The Macerich Company (MAC) is up 71% since then:

But I think that investors are calling victory too early.

REITs are mostly borrowing long-term debt, and therefore, the long end of the curve matters a lot more to them than what the Fed Funds rate is on any given day.

The bad news is that long-term interest rates have been steadily creeping back up, with the 30-year treasury (US30Y) just crossing the 5% mark once again:

To be clear, this does not spell disaster for most REITs. Most of them have strong balance sheets and trade at historically low valuations, which actually makes them quite compelling in today's environment.

However, you need to be very selective in this space because there are vast differences in the quality of balance sheets from one REIT to another.

We see a lot of investors buy into highly leveraged REITs, often overlooking how big an impact the higher interest rates could have on their cash flow as their debt comes due. As an example, Global Net Lease (GNL) is often pitched as a "value opportunity," trading at a low FFO multiple and offering a 10% dividend yield. However, it has a high debt-to-EBITDA and short maturities, and its dividend is already poorly covered. As more debt is refinanced at higher rates, I think that another dividend cut is inevitable.

And to be clear, it is not just REITs.

There are plenty of dividend stocks that seem compelling on the surface due to their low valuations and high yields, but their balance sheets are ill-prepared to face even just another year or two of higher interest rates.

More and more debt is coming due, and the impact of refinancing could come as a shock to some investors in the coming quarters.

AT&T (T), Verizon (VZ), Altria (MO), and many other popular names will be impacted.

Trade War

The market is busy celebrating the 90-day pauses, but it seems to forget that the trade war is not over. It was only paused.

The U.S. is still fighting the rest of the world by itself, and we are starting to get an early glimpse of how it could soon impact inflation, interest rates, and the economy.

Walmart (WMT) has warned that prices will likely go up if this thing carries on.

Even in this pause phase, the average tariff rate is still far higher than it was before "liberation day," and eventually, this will either result in higher inflation or lower margins.

Most likely, a combination of both.

This also means that inflation could shoot up in the second half of the year and potentially lead to higher interest rates.

Meanwhile, the economy is also weakening because there is too uncertainty. No one knows what will happen next, and this is preventing companies from making big investment decisions. Consumers are also starting to lose confidence, resulting in less spending.

What companies will suffer from this?

I think that many more than the market realizes.

We recently shared an article on the popular dividend growth ETF called Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), in which we examined its top holdings, and we found that nearly all of them were exposed to the risks presented by this trade war. Here is what we wrote:

Coca-Cola (KO) and PepsiCo (PEP) are very recognizable brands that most foreign consumers associate with the USA. It makes it very easy to boycott, and the switching costs are low. If the price of Coca-Cola goes up significantly in China, a lot of consumers will simply switch to a lower-cost alternative that tastes similar.

The energy companies Chevron (CVX) and ConocoPhillips (COP) will suffer from the now much lower commodity prices. Energy prices have crashed to much lower level, and this will affect their profitability.

The big pharmaceutical companies like Amgen (AMGN) and Bristol (BMY) also generate a substantial portion of their revenue from foreign countries, and the current administration thinks that it is especially important to bring back their production to the US to not rely on foreign countries for medicine. That's a fair point, but it will come at a cost, likely reducing profits and making them less competitive on the global stage.

Home Depot (HD) is importing a ton of stuff from abroad, so its cost will go up, and if the economy dips into a recession, people will not move as much, let alone spend money on home renovations.

Finally, defense companies like Lockheed Martin (LMT) are crashing because the US is losing the trust of its allies, and as a result, they are also less likely to buy American weapons. As an example, President Trump recently suggested that the future American fighter jets sold abroad should be toned down in case today's allies are not allies in the future anymore. Some countries like Portugal have already responded to this by cancelling their orders, and the European Union is now planning to drastically increase its own weapons production.

Even then, the ETF itself and most of these companies keep receiving "Buy" or "Strong Buy" ratings day after day, as if this trade war did not matter.

I think that many investors are due for some nasty surprises.

AI revolution

Finally, the AI revolution is set to flip the world upside down in the coming years, and no one seems to be paying attention.

I think that the market has only recognized the first-degree effect so far, which is that chip companies like Nvidia (NVDA) and data center REITs like Digital Realty (DLR) will benefit from it.

However, we think that it is still overlooking the second-degree effects, which are just as important.

If there is one Silicon Valley VC that should listen to it, it is Vinod Khosla. He has been an early investor in lots of today's biggest tech companies, including OpenAI.

He thinks that AI will end up hurting most companies because it eliminates traditional barriers to entry, invites much more competition, and leads to significant deflation, which will ultimately lead to lower profits and company valuations.

Starting a new business has already become way easier, and with the introduction and further development of AI agents, it will soon feel as if you have the entire freelancer base of Fiverr working for you. Nearly for free.

Khosla thinks that nearly all incumbent companies will end up losing in this revolution as new, far more efficient AI-first start-ups emerge in every industry and come to disrupt the slow and expensive labor-first incumbents.

And this is not far into the future. This will all happen in the next decade or two, and it could have a profound negative impact on a lot of today's leading dividend stocks.

The best example that comes to my mind right now is SL Green Realty (SLG), a popular REIT that owns office skyscrapers in Manhattan. It is often pitched as an attractive dividend investment as it offers a 5.3% %dividend yield.

But what will happen to all these buildings as AI eliminates a big chunk of white collar jobs? These buildings are occupied by law firms, consulting companies, banks, funds, marketing agencies, etc. These are all jobs that are expected to experience huge displacements. Before AI, a law firm might have had 10 junior lawyers to draft contracts. In the future, it will be just one or two with the help of AI.

The vacancy rate for office space in NYC is already at a near all-time high, and this AI revolution surely won't help.

But there are plenty of other examples. Walgreens (WBA) and CVS (CVS) are being disrupted by AI-friendly platforms like Hims & Hers (HIMS), for instance. Walgreens, which was once one of the safest dividend stocks, has recently eliminated its dividend altogether.

The world is moving fast, and most industries will experience enormous changes in this AI revolution.

Closing Note

The high interest rates, trade war, and AI revolution have the potential to disrupt a lot of very popular dividend stocks.

Don't make the mistake of overlooking these angles, or you will pay the price.

The key in this environment is to be highly selective, and this is why we invest in only about one company out of twenty that we analyze at High Yield Investor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: QCLN: Tough Week For Clean Energy, Sell As Outlook Looks Bleak
data.description:
About 6 months ago I gave First Trust Nasdaq Clean Edge Green Energy Index Fund (NASDAQ:QCLN) a hold rating. The stock is down 6.75% since then, but has been on the rise, up 23.70% in the last month.

The ETF invests in stocks of companies involved in the clean energy field. The companies within operate in manufacturing, developing, distributing, or installing of advanced materials, energy intelligence or storage, and renewable electric generation. QCLN has $418.14 million in AUM, offers a 0.97% dividend yield, and costs investors an expense ratio of 0.59%.

With recent news from the Trump administration, it looks like the “Big Beautiful Bill” is going to drown out a lot of activity in the clean energy world. A few holdings for the ETF are already hurting prior to this news, and this index fund is too volatile in an already choppy market. I rate QCLN a sell.

The Fund

QCLN has 51 holdings, and for the most part still contains a similar top 10 in weighting. First Solar (FSLR) has moved up to over 10% of the portfolio, and the stock itself is down about 13% in the last week or so. Rivian Automotive (RIVN) and Tesla (TSLA), are prominent in the electric vehicle (EV) world, and now compose almost 19% of the portfolio. ON Semiconductor (ON) holds 7%, but has performed poorly of late, from 18% YoY revenue decline and extremely rough EPS revisions and growth expectations.

Recent Clean Energy News

The “Big Beautiful Bill” was passed on Thursday Morning by the House in a neck and neck 215-214 vote. The bill will have a major impact on individuals, families, and companies alike. Business wise, firms involved in the clean energy landscape are possibly going to be the most adversely affected. Here’s the run down on a few of QCLN’s top holdings and how they are going to be troubled.

Electric Vehicles

Starting with electric vehicles, TSLA and RIVN could be in for a world of hurt. The current electric vehicle tax credit of up to $7,500 was set to expire on December 21, 2032. With the updated bill, the end to the tax credit will be pushed to December 31, 2025, and no more EV tax credit in 2026 and beyond. Not only is the tax credit axed, but the House Transportation and Infrastructure Committee has proposed annual fees of $250 and $100 to EV and Hybrids respectively. As many Americans are already strapped for cash, it’s evident that demand for EV’s will drop without the incentives that consumers have come accustomed to.

Crazy enough, there is an even worse element for TSLA and RIVN. Q1 2024 U.S. sales were 37% lower for RIVN, and 9% less for TSLA. Most other motor companies, saw a large rise in sales YoY. All other EV’s combined for a 9% positive sales gain. EV sales have continued to rise (Pre-Big Beautiful Bill), but just not for TSLA and RIVN. TSLA European sales were down 44% in February, from the same month last year, while the European EV market was all together 24% higher in that comparison.

Solar Lanscape

Currently there is a 30% residential solar tax credit for installing solar energy systems. Under the bill, this credit will also be eliminated at the end of 2025. A huge credit for homeowners that is dispersed at the end of the year, will certainly lead to significantly lighter demand for solar in neighborhoods. This changes the overall solar landscape, but luckily for FSLR, the update to the bill is only geared toward residential for now. Majority of FSLR business comes from large scale utility projects, which right now are not affected. They are participants in the residential side slightly, and I am still leery on FSLR as things have changed quick with this administration.

Though a leader in residential solar is SolarCity, a company bought by TSLA in 2016. I foresee an extremely rough next few years for this space, as demand will fall fast, and residential solar companies are going to need to find a new business approach to stay afloat.

More Risk

There is more than enough economic risk at hand, but QCLN itself already poses a lot. With a beta of 1.52 and standard deviation more than double the average, QCLN is just too volatile. A negative Sharpe Ratio, and massively low Alpha showcases the underperformance, and an unreliable risk adjusted display. The less than 1% dividend is irrelevant under these risk circumstances. There are also hundreds of index funds with an expense ratio that is a quarter of what QCLN costs.

Conclusion

Unfortunately for QCLN, this week’s news is going to be a huge strain on the clean energy industry. There have been over 2.5 million EV sales in the United States in the last 4 years. It will be very hard for that pace to continue without government incentive for the consumer. But regardless, TSLA and RIVN are already on a heavy sales decline anyways. With auto being the highest industry exposure for QCLN this creates a tough environment. Renewable energy, like solar, is another 20% of the portfolio and the industry also getting decimated in residential. While ETFs are generally a safer investment, QCLN is high risk. The fund has been on a 4-year decline and is down 65% from early 2021. I don’t think the end is in sight yet. Sell QCLN.

data.title: Innovation In Indexing: Why Passive ETFs Matter
data.description:
For many of us, ETFs have been synonymous with passive management. Since the early 1990s, ETFs have followed in the footsteps of the most well-known passive ETF — the S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY). And even today, ETFs have a reputation for their accessibility and simplicity.

Recently, active management has brought another layer of evolution (with potentially higher returns) to the ETF world. As a result, active ETFs have seen rapid development. Most new ETF launches are now active ETFs. In fact, out of the 300+ ETFs launched this year, less than 10% are passive ETFs. We shouldn’t, however, confuse passive management with passive ideas. Passive management (i.e., index investing) continues to innovate and simplify complex investment ideas through factor-based and other proprietary weighting methodologies. It also provides investors access to new or niche asset classes, and redefines evolving industries.

Passive ETFs Do Not Lack Conviction

One of the most common misconceptions has been that active ETFs equal innovation, while passive ETFs equal neutrality. After all, most passive ETFs track indexes as part of investors’ long-term holdings. These “set and forget” strategies are straightforward, broad categories. These ETFs can have a relatively simple methodology, like the S&P 500, which is weighed by market capitalization. This is one of the biggest benefits of many ETFs — they are straightforward and work efficiently. That makes them attractive to retail investors. But it is easy to start viewing passive ETFs as “passive” in other senses. In other words, passive investing doesn’t mean investing without a strong investment thesis or investing in “plain vanilla” due to lack of ideas.

Passive ETFs and their indexes can also be innovative and support new strategies. Many use alternate weighting schemes like revenue or factor weighting. These indexes may also incorporate exclusions to create a more “active” feel. Many also use advanced optimization and other technology to run scenarios. And, finally, there are still plenty of passive ETFs that thrive in the disruptive technology space and play an important role in defining new industries or asset classes. This includes anything from thematic equities (new disruptive industries) to emerging asset classes that may be difficult to invest in outside of the ETF structure (e.g., digital assets and private markets).

Active ETFs Growing in Popularity, But Have Pros and Cons Relative to Passive ETFs

Active ETFs are managed by portfolio managers who try to outperform a benchmark index. This gives them more flexibility than traditional index funds. That flexibility can be helpful, especially in volatile markets where a flexible strategy may capture short-term opportunities. But it can be a double-edged sword. Active decisions can also lead to emotional investing or straying from a long-term strategy. Because of the added layer of management, these funds tend to come with higher expense ratios. And while investors are paying for that expertise, it doesn’t guarantee outperformance. Many active strategies still fall short of passive benchmarks over time.

Passive ETFs: Examples of Innovation

There’s still a lot of innovative ideas in passive ETFs, despite the lack of launches. The following includes just some of the examples of passive ETFs launched this year.

Factor and Other Proprietary Weighting Schemes

There have been several ETFs with fresh takes on older ideas. The Alger Russell Innovation ETF (INVN), for instance, focuses on undervalued innovative companies by starting with the well-known Russell 1000 Index, removing the bottom one-third of stocks by trailing twelve-month free cash flow margin, and ranking the remaining stocks by the amount each company spends on research and development as a percentage of its enterprise value.

Similarly, Global X released two ETFs this year which were very relevant in the current tariff environment. The Global X S&P 500 Revenue Leaders ETF (EGLE) and the Global X S&P 500 US Market Leaders Top 50 ETF (FLAG). EGLE tracks an index which targets S&P 500 companies that draw at least half their revenue from domestic sources. Similarly, FLAG also tracks U.S. revenue-focused companies, but selects a narrower pool of 50 market leaders.

Franklin has launched two ETFs — the Franklin U.S. Dividend Multiplier Index ETF (XUDV) and Franklin International Dividend Multiplier Index (XIDV). These ETFs track broad equity indexes and aim to provide a dividend yield of two or three times that of the parent index. While the multiplier may make it sound similar to leveraged products, these ETFs are not leveraged and do not use derivatives. The underlying indexes are balanced against volatility and concentration risk using a three-stage optimization process where thousands of simulations are run against market universes.

Access to New or Niche Asset Classes (Including Crypto)

On the crypto front, several products have been released, including the Bitwise Bitcoin Standard Corporations ETF (OWNB), which tracks an index of equities of corporations that hold at least 1000 bitcoin on their balance sheet and embrace bitcoin as a strategic reserve asset. I did a more in-depth review of crypto launches so far this year, which includes both active and passive ETFs, here. As shown in the figure above, spot crypto ETFs (also a form of passive ETF) have driven increased assets in passive ETFs.

The Pacer PE/VC ETF (PEVC) tracks a liquid index of publicly traded securities that replicate the risk and return characteristics of the U.S. private equity (PE) and venture capital (VC) industries. This involves optimizing exposure between two PE and VC indices at a lower cost than traditional private market funds (PEVC has an 85 basis point expense ratio).

Thematic: Defining New Industries

Several faith-based ETFs were also launched this year, including the JLENS 500 Jewish Advocacy US ETF (TOV). This ETF tracks an index that screens for companies that do not align with Jewish values, and the remaining companies are scored on their performance on three Jewish value scorecards.

Additionally, newer areas like electrification have grown in popularity due to trends in artificial intelligence. The ALPS Electrification Infrastructure ETF (ELFY) is one example of the new electrification theme. ELFY’s holdings are about 39% utilities, 31% industrials, 14% technology, and 13% energy. While thematic ETFs are often compared to industry ETFs, which generally follow GICS (or similar) classification standards, many thematic ETFs are multi-sector. The thematic indexes behind these ETFs help define these new multi-sector industries — or themes — and can create a roadmap that goes beyond traditional sector and industry classifications.

Bottom Line

While active ETFs have been gaining popularity, passive ETFs continue to play a critical role — not just as core building blocks in portfolios, but also as tools to gain exposure in emerging asset classes and disruptive industries.

It is provided as general information only and should not be taken as investment advice. Employees of VettaFi are prohibited from owning individual MLPs. For more information on VettaFi, visit VettaFi.

data.title: Axon Enterprise: Premium Multiple Expansion, Structural Multiple Risk, Initiate Sell
data.description:
Key thesis

We initiate coverage on Axon Enterprise, Inc. (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:AXON) with a Sell rating and a $608 PT. Axon Enterprise, Inc. is a leading provider of conducted energy devices, body-worn video recording devices and cloud-based digital evidence management software solutions to law enforcement and public safety agencies. While we model Axon to deliver FY25E revenues of $2.71bn (+21.5% y/y) and FY26E revenues of $3.36bn (+24.0% y/y) ahead of consensus driven by strength in software ARR (+34% y/y to $1.1bn) and ongoing segment outperformance, we believe the shares are priced for ultimately unrealistic expectations.

We base our $608 PT on 14.0x FY26E EV/Sales, as we take the view that a sustained environment of heightened real interest rates and quantitative tightening will ultimately structurally compress premium multiples for long-duration growth assets such as AXON, regardless of near-term execution. Despite the management team's strong track-record and growing TAM, we believe the shares trade with a significant premium to historical and peer levels, leaving the R/R skewed significantly to the downside if macro headwinds continue to build or revenue momentum slows.

We acknowledge the underlying execution risk (integration, competition, and internal control weaknesses); however, we believe the current valuation embeds these to the extent we can frame the R/R in our disciplined multiple framework. On balance, we believe the risk of multiple compression outweighs the potential upside from continued growth, and we initiate with a Sell rating and $608 PT.

Above-Consensus FY25/FY26 Revenue Estimates on High-Margin Software & Diversifying End-Markets

Our estimates of Axon Enterprise FY25 revenue of $2,71mn (+21.5% y/y) and FY26 revenue of $3,36mn (+24.0% y/y) compared to the Street FY25 of $2,655mn (+19.2% y/y) and FY26 of $3,265mn (+23.0% y/y). Our conviction is based on AXON's consistent outperformance (Q1 2025 revenues +31% y/y, 13 straight quarters of 25%+ growth, resulting in management's upward FY25 guide rebase) and belief that the Street models underappreciate the trajectory of the high-margin Software and Services segment, which saw ARR increase 34% y/y to $1.1bn and segment revenue in Q1 up 39% y/y, driven by rapid adoption of AI products and elevated NRR of 123%.

Accelerating TASER 10 demand (order rates 2x prior gen) and a sharp increase in international bookings (+50% sequentially in Q4 2024) and a record enterprise deal also reflect growing growth vectors. Total contracted bookings remain near all-time highs ($9.9bn as of Q1 2025), providing solid NT visibility. While robust execution and expanding TAM support a premium revenue trajectory, we acknowledge valuation risk; however, on fundamentals alone, we find substantial justification for above-Street estimates.

Valuation fragility in a macro regime shift

Axon is most sensitive to structural multiple compression risk in our view, given that the premium narrative is likely to fall apart as the macro backdrop becomes less supportive. AXON trades on a forward EV/Sales multiple well above its own 5-yr avg and, more importantly, on a forward multiple that represents an even greater premium to the Industrials median. This highlights how much a hyper-growth narrative is already baked in.

However, with Fed balance sheet runoff and elevated real rates, the new normal, the appetite for long-duration assets has clearly diminished, as seen in the Industrials sector rerating since 2022. The point here is that it's not cyclical but a structural regime change, and as quantitative tightening continues, there will be less appetite for distant cash flows, regardless of idiosyncratic execution.

Even if AXON continues to outperform on revenue and software ARR growth, the macro backdrop can drive substantial de-rating, bringing the multiple more in line with historical or peer levels. The fragility of AXON's valuation is structural and not as much tied to immediate execution, and investors should be aware that it could be a sustained drag even if the company is not making material operational mistakes.

Execution and Control Weaknesses: Underestimated Sustainable Growth Risks

It is impossible to overstate the importance of Axon's execution and control weaknesses, considering the outsized reliance on breakneck expansion from both internal innovation and headline-grabbing acquisitions to support long-term growth targets. Headline results continue to highlight impressive top-line growth, with a strong bookings pipeline, but management commentary is evidence of the fragility of the company's underlying integration efforts. For example, the integration of acquired companies (e.g., Fusus and Dedrone) is mentioned repeatedly as a catalyst for international and enterprise market expansion, yet there is scant operational detail on how the company is ensuring seamless product and cultural alignment, which we see as an important risk as Axon expands into new and diverse customer bases and geographies.

We see the lack of transparency as material, especially given the complexity of integrating disparate software, hardware, and sales teams, and the potential for mistakes that could damage Axon's "trusted technology partner" reputation, a core differentiator in the market. Additionally, Axon's shift to a new non-GAAP bookings metric, explained as a result of "changing accountants" and increasing termination-for-convenience clauses, underscores evolving internal practices that may mask true contract quality and revenue visibility.

These process changes, combined with rapid scale and integration efforts, increase the likelihood of operational missteps or regulatory scrutiny, any of which could impede Axon's ability to sustain its above-consensus growth profile and further depress its lofty valuation multiple.

Valuation

Our $608 PT for Axon Enterprise, Inc. is based on a fundamental multiple approach, applying 14.0x EV/Sales to our robust FY26E revenues of $3.36bn, a combination supported by Axon's outperformance and the backdrop of the current macro environment. While Axon's continued above-consensus revenue growth (+24.0% in FY26E), driven by software ARR (+34% y/y in FY26E) and gross margins (>60%) would normally warrant premium multiples, we believe that structural headwinds (high real interest rates & QT) will persistently constrain long-duration growth multiples.

The 14.0x multiple is a high-end premium to historical (9.6x; 99th pctile) and well above the current peer median (5.2x; 100th pctile), supported by (1) execution, (2) sector leadership, and (3) increasing TAM to legacy norms, but represents a significant de-rating from current 23.8x EV/Sales, reflecting execution risk / heightened competition/restatement of internal controls.

The market is underappreciating how quickly elevated funding costs can re-rate even best-in-class growth names. This rapidly compresses upside for names perceived as structurally shielded from macro conditions. We believe the R/R is now skewed to the downside as market conditions shift, warranting our Sell rating and multiple framework despite operational outperformance.

What could break our Sell thesis?

Our Sell rating on Axon will be proven wrong if any of the below points materialize more positively than expected. While we base our valuation on structural multiple compression for premium assets in a tighter macro environment, continued strength in Axon's growth drivers (e.g., outsized adoption of new AI-enhanced platforms, such as Draft One, Axon Assistant, etc., and faster-than-expected international and enterprise adoption) could sustain above-consensus revenue momentum and hence operating leverage / further margin expansion.

Axon's bookings visibility and customer retention (123% NRR, > $9.9bn in bookings backlog) should prove somewhat resilient to cyclical macro risks if public safety and justice technology budgets are less rate sensitive than assumed in our base case. Most notably, our Sell thesis could be broken if the public sector demand for digital evidence and AI-powered real-time operations accelerates (due to either greater regulatory mandate or competitive disruptions elsewhere) at a faster-than-expected rate, supporting premium multiples on durable top-line outperformance.

Catalysts for a re-rate would include further ARR upside, additional margin expansion (particularly AI-driven), and evidence that quantitative tightening is less of a headwind to law enforcement and municipal procurement than history might suggest.

Conclusion

While we believe Axon Enterprise's execution and stellar growth profile remain notable, we see valuation as topping out at best-case outcomes and leaving investors exposed to structural multiple compression in a higher-rate market. We find risk/reward to be asymmetric to the downside, with our analysis indicating even sustained outperformance may not shield shares from a broader derating in growth assets. Our cautious stance should keep investors attentive to the macro headwinds and Axon's ability to deliver additional software-led operating leverage, before turning long here.

data.title: PFN: Suppressed By Elevated Interest Rate Environment
data.description:
Overview

I've grown quite fond of debt focused investments because of the income and price stability they can provide during periods of elevated market volatility. The strategic use of debt investments can be an efficient way to generate income within a diverse portfolio. PIMCO Income Strategy Fund II (NYSE:PFN) operates as a closed end fund that aims to provide attractive total returns and capital preservation through its diverse portfolio of debt investments. The fund has been around since 2004, meaning that we have over two decades worth of operating history to reference. PFN also has a management fee of 0.83%.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that PFN's price has decreased by about 3.1%. When including the distributions paid out to shareholders over the same time frame, the total return jumps up to 10.1%. The main appeal of holding shares of a fund like PFN is the generous starting dividend yield of 11.9%. This double-digit yield makes it an enticing choice for investors that want to put an emphasis on income generation within their portfolio.

While I like the idea and purpose that a fund like PFN serves, the appeal is highly dependent on general market and macro-economic conditions. For instance, it seems like PFN's valuation currently remains suppressed due to the higher interest rate environment that we remain in. With so much uncertainty around the outlook of interest rates, there's a strong possibility that PFN's price and NAV growth continue to disappoint in the short term. Patient investors may be able to benefit by waiting for the storm to pass, but there's no telling how long conditions will remain unfavorable. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying fund strategy that is utilized to generate earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to PFN's most recent fact sheet, the fund has total managed assets of $719.4M that is spread across a diverse range of income assets. For instance, the majority of the fund is allocated to High Yield Credit investments that make up 36.3% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Emerging Market and Non-USD Developed investments, accounting for 15.2% and 12.5% of assets respectively. The combination of these three sectors makes up approximately 64% of the total fund, so we can assume that these will be the biggest drivers of daily price changes and total return potential.

The fund chooses its investments based on several components, like the ability for capital appreciation, attractive company-specific metrics, or the ability to preserve principal. PFN will allocate its assets between a mix of floating and fixed rate debt securities and will blend its focus across different credit ratings. However, the overall success of debt investments is typically reliant on the interest rate environment. Floating rate debt investments have the potential to generate elevated income for funds since the cost of debt is higher.

In an effort to mitigate any sector-specific risks, PFN spreads its investments out across a diverse range of industries as well. This can help the fund provide better resilience through any unexpected sector-specific challenges. For instance, Technology investments make up 9.7% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Wirelines and Healthcare investments, making up 8.4% and 7.9% of assets respectively.

As part of the fund's strategy, PFN utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify returns when an attractive opportunity presents itself. Leverage use is relatively conservative at only 12.33% of assets. I think that this amount of leverage is mild and doesn't significantly increase the risk profile of the fund. Investors must consider the risk that leverage can also amplify losses if allocated towards an investment that doesn't perform as planned.

The fund's strategy has ultimately led to the price declining by more than 28.6% over the last decade. However, this was offset by total returns, including distributions, amounting to 103.8%. Whether or not this sort of performance is ideal for investors is highly depend on an individual's underlying goal and investment objective. If you are an investor that seeks total return, then PFN would not be a good fit. Conversely, investors that simply want a place to park their money and generate income, PFN could be a suitable choice.

Valuation

Since PFN operates as a closed end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying net assets. Therefore, PFN currently trades at a slight premium to NAV of about 3.14%. For reference, the PFN has traded at an average premium to NAV of about 3.3% over the last three year period, so PFN remains pretty close to its normal range. Looking back over the last ten years below, we can see that PFN frequently trades in the premium to NAV territory, signaling that the market has a high amount of confidence in the fund's ability to generate positive returns.

To get better clarity into the fund's details, I reviewed the most recent semi-annual report. Net investment income amounted to $0.42 per share while net realized landed at $0.27 per share for the first half of the fiscal year. Looking back on prior years, we can see that net investment income levels have typically remained in a consistent range.

Conversely, net realized gains have been more variable and seem to be more in sync with general market conditions. Looking at the numbers, it seems like the income received from PFN could offer some resilience to market declines, but PFN's NAV per share still remains below its pre-pandemic level. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2019: $9.91 per share
  • 2020: $8.53 per share
  • 2021: $9.42 per share
  • 2022: $7.38 per share
  • 2023: $6.85 per share
  • 2024: $6.93 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $7.20 per share

A likely contributor to the fund's inability to regain its positive momentum back above its pre-pandemic level is the current interest rate environment. While higher interest rates can be a great catalyst for elevated income generation from borrowers, it also increases the risk profile for these sort of debt focused funds that have a large exposure to borrowers with poor credit ratings.

Risk

The higher interest rates are more impactful when a fund has a larger exposure to riskier loans. For reference, borrowers with a credit rating below BBB are considered to be below investment grade. Borrowers with a poor credit rating typically have less consistent cash flows, are more reliant on debt financing to support operations, and may not have large cash balances to help them navigate headwinds. According to a breakdown of the fund, PFN has a sizable exposure to investments that have poor credit ratings. The breakdown is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 58.56%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - CCC): 34.62%
  • Not Rated: 6.82%

Although the majority of PFN's exposure is to investment rated borrowers, the price has still demonstrated some sensitivity to changes in the interest rate environment. Looking at the chart below, we can see how PFN's price rapidly increased throughout 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were cut to near zero levels in response to the pandemic. A low cost of debt means that borrowers were more likely to thrive since debt didn't have a large impact on operating margins. A lower interest rate environment also make higher yielding debt investments more attractive.

Conversely, we can see that PFN's price rapidly deteriorated throughout 2022 and 2023 as the Fed aggressively hiked interest rates. A higher cost of borrowing typically leads to a higher rate of borrow defaults. Since PFN has a sizeable exposure to borrowers with below investment grade credit ratings, the risk of defaults make the fund a bit less attractive. S&P Global estimates that the rate of leverage loan defaults could increase by December of 2025 if the interest rate environment doesn't improve.

Unfortunately, it doesn't seem like markets are likely to experience interest rate cuts over the remainder of 2025. The Fed has kept rates unchanged for their third meeting as they continue to monitor economic data like inflation. Inflationary data still remains higher than anticipated and the ripple effects that President Trump's tariff proposals are still to be determined. A higher tariff could ultimately lead to higher inflation, which would reduce the chances that we see interest rate cuts happen. Not to mention, the unemployment rate has trended upwards over the last twelve months and consumer spending habits have been changing in response to the higher costs of living.

Dividend

As of the most recently declared monthly distribution of $0.0718 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.9%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve, we get an estimated annual payout amount of $0.8616 per share. Looking back over the last decade, PFN managed to provide consistent payouts until they were reduced in the middle of 2021. Since then, the fund has been inconsistent at generating positive earnings that out pace the annual distribution amount. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2019: $0.82 per share
  • 2020: ($0.49) per share
  • 2021: $1.81 per share
  • 2022: ($1.25) per share
  • 2023: $0.31 per share
  • 2024: $0.88 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.69 per share

As you can see, there were only two instances over the last six years where PFN was able to efficiently out earn its distribution. This failure to support the distribution with earnings can also be a contributor to why the fund's NAV hasn't been able to regain any positive growth back above its pre-pandemic level. From this perspective, I wouldn't be surprised if PFN reduced its distribution if performance doesn't improve. A reduction would help reduce the NAV deterioration experienced.

It's also worth noting that the distributions are typically made up of a combination of net investment income and net realized gains. Therefore, distributions are usually classified as ordinary dividends, which are taxed at the highest levels. If you do want to maintain a position in PFN, it would be best utilized within a tax advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, PFN may be a solid income fund that can provide diverse debt exposure. However, the current timing around the appeal is not ideal. The higher interest rate environment has weighed on the price and if there aren't any interest rate cuts to take place over the remainder of 2025, PFN's NAV could remain suppressed. The fund has also demonstrated inconsistent earnings over recent years, which makes it difficult to remain confident in the sustainability of the distribution. Perhaps the fund will offer a better value proposition when interest rates are lowered.

data.title: Commodities: Noise Around OPEC+ Production Increases
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Ewa Manthey

Energy - OPEC+ output policy

The oil market is under renewed pressure as noise builds around what OPEC+ will do with their July output levels. There are reports suggesting the group is considering another large supply increase, similar to those in May and June output. This would cement the shift in policy from the group - moving from defending prices to defending market share. In our balance sheet, we assume that OPEC+ will go ahead with a 411k b/d supply increase for July. Therefore, our price forecasts will remain unchanged if an increase of this size is confirmed at the beginning of next month. We currently forecast Brent to average US$59/bbl in the fourth quarter. The front-month ICE Brent timespread has also come under pressure, falling from a backwardation of US$0.74/bbl at the start of the week to around US$0.50/bbl.

Meanwhile, following a G7 summit in Canada, finance ministers have threatened further sanctions against Russia if no progress is made towards a peace deal with Ukraine. In addition, the EU is throwing around the idea of lowering the G7 price cap for Russian oil to US$50/bbl from US$60/bbl. Russian Urals are currently trading at around US$55/bbl, which allows Western shipping services to be used in the trade of this oil.

US natural gas prices sold off yesterday, with NYMEX Henry Hub settling 3.4% lower on the day. This was after Energy Information Administration data showed that US natural gas storage increased by 120 billion cubic feet over the last week. This was slightly above market expectations and well above the 5-year average for an 87 bcf increase. It leaves total gas storage at 2.375 tcf, 3.9% above the 5-year average.

Agriculture - Looser corn balance sheet

In its monthly update, the International Grains Council (IGC) raised its 2025/26 global corn output forecasts and cut its demand forecast. Estimated global ending stocks are rising from 281mt to 284mt. For wheat, the council left 2025/26 production estimates unchanged. However, it lowered demand estimates marginally, raising global ending stock estimates from 260mt to 262mt. Finally, for soybeans, global ending stock estimates for 2025/26 were cut from 83mt to 81mt amid expectations for stronger demand.
